title: Supportive or exhausting: How resources synergize in network embedding innovation process to improve sustainability
abstract: As sustainability improvement becomes increasingly complex and uncertain, firms are increasingly dependent on networked innovation capability to integrate dispersed knowledge and technologies from external entities. However, the transformation from networked innovation capability to sustainability involves a procedural mechanism that requires effective synergy among limited multidimensional financial and digital resources. This procedural mechanism and the role of resource synergy within it remain underexplored. Based on network embeddedness theory, innovation value chain theory, and resource synergy theory, this study constructs an integrated “Networked Innovation Capabilities-Process-Resources” synergy framework and employs stepwise regression analysis on a sample of 165 new energy vehicle firms. Results show that: (1) Networked knowledge innovation capability promotes sustainability through a procedural mechanism involving networked technology innovation capability and market value. (2) Multidimensional financial resources exert heterogeneous effects across different links of the procedural mechanism, with financial leverage and profitability showing significantly positive moderating effects, while operational efficiency shows no significant effect. (3) Multidimensional digital resources exert dual supportive or exhausting influences on the synergistic effects of financial resources, where digital development orientation provides consistent support, whereas digital software and hardware investment exhibit both supportive and exhausting effects contingent upon the specific link of the procedural mechanism. This study elucidates how networked innovation capability affects sustainability through a procedural mechanism and how diverse yet limited resources synergize in this process. It provides theoretical and practical insights for firms and governments to promote networked innovation capability and optimize the allocation of financial and digital resources in innovation process for sustainability.

title: Co-creating a digital platform for smart working in healthcare: reconfiguring social exchange in internal digital transformation
authors: Lucas Miguel Alencar de Morais Correia
abstract: Digital technologies have profoundly transformed healthcare, a phenomenon commonly referred to as Healthcare 4.0. Among these technologies, digital platforms stand out as modular systems that evolve through collaborative and co-creative processes. However, limited attention has been given to how healthcare professionals participate in platform co-creation, how social exchange mechanisms sustain this process, and how tensions emerge from the coexistence of healthcare routines and digital transformation. Drawing on Social Exchange Theory (SET), we examine how trust, commitment, reciprocity, power, and sociotechnical tensions shape internal healthcare platform co-creation. We conducted a qualitative case study in a Brazilian teaching hospital that internalized the development of its primary information system and adopted a co-creative approach in which healthcare professionals assumed Product Owner roles for different platform modules. The empirical investigation combined 25 interviews, participant observation, and document analysis over seven months. The findings show that internal healthcare platform co-creation constitutes a distinct SET configuration in which trust depends on process visibility and responsiveness, reciprocity is associated with professional recognition and shared organizational outcomes, and power is shaped by internal hierarchies and asymmetries in technological expertise. Product Owners emerge as hybrid sociotechnical actors who mediate between healthcare work practices and technical development. Although co-creation enhances smart working by aligning digital solutions with professionals’ needs, it also generates persistent tensions related to technostress, cognitive overload, and competing attention demands between patient care and platform interaction. We argue that these tensions are inherent to healthcare digital transformation and must be continuously managed rather than eliminated.

title: Predicting Local Innovation through AI and geospatial models: Revisiting the Innovation District Theory
abstract: This paper aims to deepen the understanding of variations in innovation performance across neighbourhoods within a city. Specifically, the study investigates the characteristics that influence innovation generation at the sub-city level to provide insights that would bridge the gap between national, regional, and local innovation policies. It aims to revisit the traditional Innovation District Theory in the post-AI era by proposing an innovation district model that shifts the focus from supply chains to innovation diffusion, moving beyond characterizing innovation districts solely by business metrics. To do it, we leverage an empirically merged dataset to explore well-established and newly introduced enabling factors of innovation generation. First, we employ a random forest algorithm to predict innovation performance using patent data from the census zip code tabulation areas (ZCTAs) across the New York metro area. This approach highlights significant neighbourhood assets associated with high innovation performance. Secondly, machine learning and deep learning models are trained and tested on ZCTAs from New York state with high predictive accuracy. Our findings show that when trained with neighbourhood assets and tested on New York state ZCTAs demonstrate high predictive accuracy, explaining 60% to 70% of the variation in innovation performance ten years later using specific neighbourhood asset variables. Additionally, when trained on data from the New York central metro area tested on data from Boston's central metro area, the accuracy remains relatively stable, suggesting an association between the assessed neighbourhood assets and innovation performance across these two US central metro areas. In the end, we introduce a newly constructed index, the Local Innovation Potential (LIP), designed to a neighbourhood’s innovation potential over the same period, in both states. These results reinforce the importance of key early neighbourhood-level characteristics strongly associated with high long-term innovation performance and provide a tool with practical implications for urban planning as well as for innovation policy design and implementation.

title: Responsible Innovation at the Molecular Level: Collaboration, Networks, and Commercialization in Green Chemistry
abstract: ABSTRACT Academic Summary Responsible innovation (RI) holds profound promise for addressing many pressing problems by injecting specific and forward‐looking principles into innovation processes. However, central elements of RI frameworks, such as the prioritization of deliberation and transparency, can create tension when applied in market‐oriented domains where firms often seek competitive advantage through innovation. To help address this tension, we examine how firms' collaboration networks impact RI outcomes. We draw on research in collaborative innovation at the science‐industry interface to develop hypotheses linking specific patterns of collaboration in terms of network structure and composition to invention and commercialization outcomes. We test our hypotheses in the field of green chemistry (a subfield of chemistry that seeks to prevent environmental problems before they occur by (re)designing chemicals and chemical production processes at a molecular level) using two distinct data sources: green chemistry patents and nominations for the Presidential Green Chemistry Challenge Awards. Our findings show stark differences in the relationship between network attributes and invention versus commercialization outcomes, respectively. These differences address tensions in applying RI in a business context by showing how more nuanced approaches to collaborating can lead to better outcomes aligned with specific RI goals, including avoiding harms and promoting sustainability. Managerial Summary Shepherding innovations from early‐stage discoveries to market‐ready products and processes is a complex and fraught endeavor. It also lies at the core of many firms' strategies for seeking and sustaining advantage in competitive markets. Responsible innovation (RI) research recognizes the role firms play in developing and scaling innovative solutions to pressing problems, yet incorporating RI principles into this already daunting process creates tension. For example, deliberation and transparency are central principles of RI, yet these can also impinge innovative capacity and erode competitive advantage. In this study, we help address this tension by examining specific approaches to collaborative innovation efforts at the science‐industry interface and map these to specific outcomes valued by firms: patented inventions and commercialized products. We anchor our analysis in the field of green chemistry (a subfield within chemistry that seeks to prevent environmental problems before they occur by (re)designing chemicals and chemical production processes at a molecular level). Our results suggest that the tensions in applying RI in a business context may be less intractable than they initially appear and that firms seeking to be responsive to RI principles while also strengthening their competitive advantage can do more than simply “strike a balance” between these objectives.

title: Normative entrainment through affect in national artificial intelligence strategies
abstract: Policy documents guide technological transformations. Prior research on national artificial intelligence (AI) strategies has examined how these texts articulate governance values and principles, but how they promote identification, urgency, and action is less well understood. This gap is especially striking given arguments that national AI strategies are typically written for policy professionals. We show that these documents are also widely used as publicly accessible reference points in debates on responsible AI. Building on the narrative turn in public policy scholarship, we analyse 22 European national AI strategies. We find that AI strategies create normative entrainment through affect, constituted by three narratives: (1) establishing normative orientations by ascribing societal values to AI; (2) anchoring these orientations by invoking social emotions that embed AI in cultural identity and everyday life; and (3) promoting implementation by juxtaposing moral emotions such as fear and hope while mobilising urgency, leadership, and responsibility. By specifying how emotional narratives are woven into policy texts, this framework contributes to research on the communicative construction of AI policy and offers implications for responsible digital transformation.
